volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Ghost (Section 1.3.172 - Section 1.3.258)

sections.label: Section 1.3.172
sections.original: Quid nunc ?
sections.translation: Well now ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.172b
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: What is it ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.172c
sections.original: Quin me aspice et contempla , ut haec me deceat .
sections.translation: Why look at me and examine , how this becomes me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.173
sections.original: Virtute formae id evenit , te ut deceat quidquid habeas .
sections.translation: Thanks to your good looks , it happens that whatever you put on becomes you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.174
sections.original: Ergo ob istuc verbum te , Scapha , donabo ego hodie aliqui ,
sections.translation: Now then , for that expression , Scapha , I ’ ll make you some present or other to-day ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.175
sections.original: neque patiar te istanc gratiis laudasse , quae placet mi .
sections.translation: and I won ’ t allow you to have praised her for nothing who is so pleasing to me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.176
sections.original: Nolo ego te adsentari mihi .
sections.translation: I don ’ t want you to flatter me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.176b
sections.original: Nimis tú quidem stulta es mulier .
sections.translation: Really you are a very simple woman . Come now , would you rather be censured undeservedly , than be praised with truth ? Upon my faith , for my own part , even though undeservedly , I ’ d much rather be praised than be found fault with with reason , or that other people should laugh at my appearance .

sections.label: Section 1.3.181
sections.original: Ego verum amo , verum volo dici mihi : mendacem odi .
sections.translation: I love the truth ; I wish the truth to be told me ; I detest a liar .

sections.label: Section 1.3.182
sections.original: Ita tu me ames , ita Philolaches tuos té amet , ut venusta es .
sections.translation: So may you love me , and so may your Philolaches love you , how charming you are .

sections.label: Section 1.3.183
sections.original: Quid ais , scelesta ? quo modo adiurasti ? ita ego istam amarem ?
sections.translation: How say you , you hussy ? In what words did you adjure ? So may I love her ? Why wasn ’ t So may she love me added as well ? I revoke the present .

sections.label: Section 1.3.185
sections.original: periisti : quod promiseram tibi donum , perdidisti .
sections.translation: What I just now promised you is done for ; you have lost the present .

sections.label: Section 1.3.186
sections.original: Equidem pol miror tam catam , tam docilem te et bene doctam
sections.translation: Troth , for my part I am surprised that you , a person so knowing , so clever , and so well educated , are not aware that you are acting foolishly .

sections.label: Section 1.3.187b
sections.original: Quin mone quaéso , si quid erro .
sections.translation: Then give me your advice , I beg , if I have done wrong in anything .

sections.label: Section 1.3.188
sections.original: Tu ecastor erras , quae quidem illum expectes unum atque illi
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you certainly do wrong , in setting your mind upon him alone , in fact , and humouring him in particular in this way and slighting other men .

sections.label: Section 1.3.190
sections.original: matronae , non meretricium est unum inservire amantem .
sections.translation: It ’ s the part of a married woman , and not of courtesans , to be devoted to a single lover .

sections.label: Section 1.3.191
sections.original: Pro Iuppiter , nam quod malum versatur meae domi illud ?
sections.translation: O Jupiter ! Why , what pest is this that has befallen my house ? May all the Gods and Goddesses destroy me in the worst of fashions , if I don ’ t kill this old hag with thirst , and hunger , and cold .

sections.label: Section 1.3.194
sections.original: Nolo ego mihi male te , Scapha , praecipere .
sections.translation: I don ’ t want you , Scapha , to be giving me bad advice .

sections.label: Section 1.3.194b
sections.original: Stulta es plane ,
sections.translation: You are clearly a simpleton , in thinking that he ’ ll for everlasting be your friend and well-wisher . I warn you of that ; he ’ ll forsake you by reason of age and satiety .

sections.label: Section 1.3.197
sections.original: Non spero .
sections.translation: I hope not .

sections.label: Section 1.3.197b
sections.original: Insperata accidunt magis saépe quam quae speres .
sections.translation: Things which you don ’ t hope happen more frequently than things which you do hope . In fine , if you cannot be persuaded by words to believe this to be the truth , judge of my words from facts ; consider this instance , who I now am , and who I once was .

sections.label: Section 1.3.200
sections.original: nihilo ego quam nunc tu ⋯ amata sum ; átque uni modo gessi morem :
sections.translation: No less than your are now , was I once beloved , and I devoted myself to one , who , faith , when with age this head changed its hue , forsook and deserted me . Depend on it , the same will happen to yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.3.203
sections.original: Vix comprimor , quin involem illi in oculos stimulatrici .
sections.translation: I can scarcely withhold myself from flying at the eyes of this mischief-maker .

sections.label: Section 1.3.204
sections.original: Solam ille me soli sibi suo sumptu liberavit :
sections.translation: I am of opinion that I ought to keep myself alone devoted to him ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.205
sections.original: illi me soli censeo esse oportere opsequentem .
sections.translation: since to myself alone has he given freedom for himself alone .

sections.label: Section 1.3.206
sections.original: Pro di immortales , mulierem lepidam et pudico ingenio .
sections.translation: O ye immortal Gods ! what a charming woman , and of a disposition how chaste ! By heaven , ’ tis excellently done , and I ’ m rejoiced at it , that it is for her sake I ’ ve got nothing left .

sections.label: Section 1.3.208
sections.original: Inscita ecastor tu quidem es .
sections.translation: On my word you really are silly .

sections.label: Section 1.3.208b
sections.original: Quapropter ?
sections.translation: For what reason ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.208c
sections.original: Quae istuc cures ,
sections.translation: Because you care for this , whether he loves you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.209b
sections.original: Cur obsecro non curem ?
sections.translation: Prithee , why should I not care for it ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.209c
sections.original: Libera es iam .
sections.translation: You now are free .

sections.label: Section 1.3.210
sections.original: tu iam quod quaerebas habes : ille té nisi amabit ultro ,
sections.translation: You ’ ve now got what you wanted ; if he didn ’ t still love you , as much money as he gave for your liberty , he ’ d lose .

sections.label: Section 1.3.212
sections.original: Perii hercle , ni ego illam pessumis exemplis enicasso .
sections.translation: Heavens , I ’ m a dead man if I don ’ t torture her to death after the most shocking fashion . That evil-persuading enticer to vice is corrupting this damsel .

sections.label: Section 1.3.214
sections.original: Numquam ego illi possum gratiam referre ut meritust de me .
sections.translation: Scapha , I can never return him sufficient thanks for what he deserves of me ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.215
sections.original: Scapha , id tu mihi ne suadeas , ut illúm minoris pendam .
sections.translation: don ’ t you be persuading me to esteem him less .

sections.label: Section 1.3.216
sections.original: At hoc únum facito cogites : si illum inservibis solum
sections.translation: But take care and reflect upon this one thing , if you devote yourself to him alone , while now you are at this youthful age , you ’ ll be complaining to no purpose in your aged years .

sections.label: Section 1.3.218
sections.original: In anginam ego nunc me velim verti , ut veneficae illi
sections.translation: I could wish myself this instant changed into a quinsy , that I might seize the throat of that old witch , and put an end to the wicked mischief-maker .

sections.label: Section 1.3.220
sections.original: Eundem ánimum oportet nunc mihi esse gratum , ut impetravi ,
sections.translation: It befits me now to have the same grateful feelings since I obtained it , as formerly before I acquired it , when I used to lavish caresses upon him .

sections.label: Section 1.3.222
sections.original: Divi me faciant quod volunt , ni ob istam orationem
sections.translation: May the Gods do towards me what they please , if for that speech I don ’ t make you free over again , and if I don ’ t torture Scapha to death .

sections.label: Section 1.3.224
sections.original: Si tibi sat acceptum est fore tibi victum sempiternum
sections.translation: If you are quite assured that you will have a provision to the end ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.225
sections.original: atque illum amatorem tibi proprium futurum in vita ,
sections.translation: and that this lover will be your own for life , I think that you ought to devote yourself to him alone , and assume the character of a wife [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.3.227
sections.original: Vt fama est homini , exin solet pecuniam invenire .
sections.translation: Just as a person ’ s character is , he ’ s in the habit of finding means accordingly ; if I keep a good character for myself I shall be rich enough .

sections.label: Section 1.3.229
sections.original: Siquidem hércle vendundust pater , venibit multo potius ,
sections.translation: By my troth , since selling there must be , my father shall be sold much sooner

sections.label: Section 1.3.230
sections.original: quam te me vivo umquam sinam egere aut mendicare .
sections.translation: than , while I ’ m alive , I ’ ll ever permit you to be in want or go a-begging .

sections.label: Section 1.3.231
sections.original: Quid illís futurum est ceteris qui té amant ?
sections.translation: What ’ s to become of the rest of those who are in love with you ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.231b
sections.original: Magis amabunt ,
sections.translation: They ’ ll love me the more when they see me displaying gratitude to one who has done me services .

sections.label: Section 1.3.233
sections.original: Vtinam nunc meus emortuos pater ad me nuntietur ,
sections.translation: I do wish that news were brought me now that my father ’ s dead , that I might disinherit myself of my property , and that she might be my heir .

sections.label: Section 1.3.235
sections.original: Iam ista quidem absumpta res erit : dies nóctesque estur bibitur ,
sections.translation: This property of his will certainly soon be at an end ; day and night there ’ s eating and drinking , and no one displays thriftiness ; ’ tis downright cramming [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.3.237
sections.original: In te hercle certumst principe ut sim parcus experiri ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ m determined to make trial on yourself for the first to be thrifty ; for you shall neither eat nor drink anything at my house for the next ten days .

sections.label: Section 1.3.239
sections.original: Si quid tu in illum bene voles loqui , id loqui licebit :
sections.translation: If you choose to say anything good about him , you shall be at liberty to say it ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.240
sections.original: nec recte si illi dixeris , iam ecastor vapulabis .
sections.translation: if you speak otherwise than well , on my word you shall have a beating instantly .

sections.label: Section 1.3.241
sections.original: Edepol si summo Ióvi bovi eo argénto sacruficassem ,
sections.translation: Upon my faith , if I had paid sacrifice to supreme Jove with that money which I gave for her liberty , never could I have so well employed it . Do see , how , from her very heart ’ s core , she loves me ! Oh , I ’ m a fortunate man ; I ’ ve liberated in her a patron to plead my cause for me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.245
sections.original: Video te nihili pendere prae Philolache omnis homines .
sections.translation: I see that , compared with Philolaches , you disregard all other men ; now , that on his account I mayn ’ t get a beating , I ’ ll agree with you in preference , if you are quite satisfied that he will always prove a friend to you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.248
sections.original: Cédo mi speculum et cum ornamentis arculam actutum , Scapha ,
sections.translation: Give me the mirror [*] , and the casket with my trinkets , directly , Scapha , that I may be quite dressed when Philolaches , my delight , comes here .

sections.label: Section 1.3.250
sections.original: Mulier quae se suamque aetatem spernit , speculo ei usus est :
sections.translation: A woman who neglects herself and her youthful age has occasion for a mirror ; what need of a mirror have you , who yourself are in especial a mirror for a mirror .

sections.label: Section 1.3.252
sections.original: Ob istuc verbum , ne nequiquam , Scapha , tam lepide dixeris ,
sections.translation: For that expression , Scapha , that you mayn ’ t have said anything so pretty in vain , I ’ ll to-day give something for your savings — to you , my Philematium .

sections.label: Section 1.3.254
sections.original: Suo quique loco ( viden ?) capillus satis compositust commode .
sections.translation: Will you see that each hair is nicely arranged in its own place ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.255
sections.original: Vbi tu commoda es , capillum commodum esse credito .
sections.translation: When you yourself are so nice , do believe that your hair must be nice .

sections.label: Section 1.3.256
sections.original: Vah , quid illá pote peius quicquam muliere memorarier ?
sections.translation: Out upon it ! what worse thing can possibly be spoken of than this woman ? Now the jade ’ s a flatterer , just now she was all contradictory .

sections.label: Section 1.3.258
sections.original: Cedo cerussam .
sections.translation: Hand me the ceruse [*] .
